steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the main idea of the passage. Defoe contrasts a sermon—which "extends no farther than the strength of memory can convey it"—with a printed book, which "conveys its contents for ages to come, to the eternity of mortal time." The central claim is that print outlasts speech.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Words committed to print have a greater permanence than messages that are merely spoken aloud.: Captures Defoe's direct contrast between the fleeting sermon and the enduring printed record. ✓
  • Choice B — People are less likely to forget a message when they hear it spoken aloud than they are when they read it in print.: Reverses the passage's argument—Defoe says the spoken sermon fades with memory, not that it's better retained. ✗
  • Choice C — Unless a spoken message is delivered by an expert, it can be safely ignored.: Introduces expertise, which the passage never mentions. ✗
  • Choice D — Most authors have little hope of being remembered well past their lifetimes.: Contradicts the passage—Defoe says books "convey contents for ages to come" even when the author is forgotten; the book's permanence is his point, not pessimism about authors. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
